is a network, often of [triangles](/wiki/Triangle "Triangle"), that are measured precisely by techniques of *control surveying*, such as terrestrial [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") or [satellite geodesy](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy "Satellite geodesy"). It is also known as a **geodetic network**, **reference network**, **control point network**, or simply **control network**.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Network of points with known positions used in geodesy and surveying

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Geodetic_control_network "Special:EditPage/Geodetic control network") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Geodetic control network"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Geodetic+control+network%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Geodetic+control+network%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Geodetic+control+network%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Geodetic+control+network%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Geodetic+control+network%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Geodetic+control+network%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/%C3%9Cbersicht_der_Stationen.PNG/250px-%C3%9Cbersicht_der_Stationen.PNG)](/wiki/File:%C3%9Cbersicht_der_Stationen.PNG)

Network of reference stations used by Austrian Positioning Service (APOS)

A **geodetic control network** is a network, often of [triangles](/wiki/Triangle "Triangle"), that are measured precisely by techniques of *control surveying*, such as terrestrial [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") or [satellite geodesy](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy "Satellite geodesy"). It is also known as a **geodetic network**, **reference network**, **control point network**, or simply **control network**.

A geodetic control network consists of *[geodetic markers](/wiki/Geodetic_marker "Geodetic marker")*, which are stable, identifiable points or vertices with published coordinate values derived from observations that tie the points together.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In the U.S., there is a national control network called the [National Spatial Reference System](/wiki/National_Spatial_Reference_System "National Spatial Reference System") (NSRS). Many organizations may contribute information to the geodetic control network.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) In the United Kingdom, the [Ordnance Survey](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey "Ordnance Survey") maintains the *OS Net* network.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The higher-order (high precision, usually millimeter-to-decimeter on a scale of continents) control points are normally defined in both space and time using global or space techniques, and are used for "lower-order" points to be tied into. The lower-order control points are normally used for [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), [construction](/wiki/Construction "Construction") and [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation"). The scientific discipline that deals with the establishing of coordinates of points in a control network is called [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy").

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Applications")]

### Cartography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cartography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/1848_U.S._Coast_Survey_Map_of_New_England_-_Geographicus_-_NewEngland-uscs-1848.jpg/250px-1848_U.S._Coast_Survey_Map_of_New_England_-_Geographicus_-_NewEngland-uscs-1848.jpg)](/wiki/File:1848_U.S._Coast_Survey_Map_of_New_England_-_Geographicus_-_NewEngland-uscs-1848.jpg)

Example of triangle network and its application in cartography

Main article: [Georeferencing](/wiki/Georeferencing "Georeferencing")

After a cartographer registers key points in a digital map to the real world coordinates of those points on the ground, the map is then said to be "in control". Having a base map and other data in geodetic control means that they will overlay correctly.

When map layers are not in control, it requires extra work to adjust them to line up, which introduces additional error.
Those real world coordinates are generally in some particular [map projection](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection"), unit, and [geodetic datum](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Construction and engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Construction and engineering")]

Survey control networks are used for different phases of engineering and construction. Applications, according to the [Chartered Institution of Civil Engineering Surveyors](/wiki/Chartered_Institution_of_Civil_Engineering_Surveyors "Chartered Institution of Civil Engineering Surveyors"), include:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* capturing topographic data,
* construction setting-out,
* as-built verification,
* infrastructure maintenance, and
* deformation monitoring.

An example application is the accurate and robust positioning of [tunnel boring machines](/wiki/Tunnel_boring_machines "Tunnel boring machines").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Measurement techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Measurement techniques")]

### Terrestrial techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Terrestrial techniques")]

Further information: [Geodesy § Positioning](/wiki/Geodesy#Positioning "Geodesy")

#### Triangulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Triangulation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/WORLDWIDE_GEOMETRIC_SATELLITE_TRIANGULATION_NETWORK%2C_BC-4_CAMERAS.GIF/250px-WORLDWIDE_GEOMETRIC_SATELLITE_TRIANGULATION_NETWORK%2C_BC-4_CAMERAS.GIF)](/wiki/File:WORLDWIDE_GEOMETRIC_SATELLITE_TRIANGULATION_NETWORK,_BC-4_CAMERAS.GIF)

Worldwide BC-4 camera geometric satellite triangulation network

Main article: [Triangulation (surveying)](/wiki/Triangulation_(surveying) "Triangulation (surveying)")

In "classical geodesy" (up to the sixties) control networks were established by [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation "Triangulation") using measurements of [angles](/wiki/Angle "Angle") and of some spare distances. The precise orientation to the [geographic north](/wiki/Geographic_north "Geographic north") is achieved through methods of [geodetic astronomy](/wiki/Geodetic_astronomy "Geodetic astronomy"). The principal instruments used are [theodolites](/wiki/Theodolite "Theodolite") and [tacheometers](/wiki/Tacheometer "Tacheometer"), which nowadays are equipped with [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") distance measuring, [data bases](/wiki/Data_base "Data base"), communication systems and partly by satellite links.

#### Trilateration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Trilateration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/MY4112-Station.jpg/250px-MY4112-Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:MY4112-Station.jpg)

Control point marker placed by the US Coast and Geodetic Survey

See also: [Trilateration](/wiki/Trilateration "Trilateration")

[Electronic distance measurement](/wiki/Electronic_distance_measurement "Electronic distance measurement") (EDM) was introduced around 1960, when the [prototype](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype") instruments became small enough to be used in the field. Instead of using only sparse and much less accurate distance measurements some control networks were established or updated by using [trilateration](/wiki/Trilateration "Trilateration") more accurate distance measurements than was previously possible and no angle measurements.

EDM increased network [accuracies](/wiki/Accuracy "Accuracy") up to 1:1 million (1 cm per 10 km; today at least 10 times better), and made surveying less costly.

#### Levelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Levelling")]

See also: [Levelling](/wiki/Levelling "Levelling")

Optical levelling, which determines relative height difference between two points, can be used to determine the vertical component of control networks, particularly relative to fixed datums (such as that employed for [Ordnance Datum Newlyn](/wiki/Ordnance_Datum_Newlyn "Ordnance Datum Newlyn")). Double run level techniques may be employed to minimise error.[[8]](#cite_note-bbc-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Satellite geodesy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Satellite geodesy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/ITRF-Fundamental-u.Satellitenstationen%2C46_po.jpg/250px-ITRF-Fundamental-u.Satellitenstationen%2C46_po.jpg)](/wiki/File:ITRF-Fundamental-u.Satellitenstationen,46_po.jpg)

International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRF) reference stations

Main article: [Satellite geodesy](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy "Satellite geodesy")

The geodetic use of [satellites](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") began around the same time. By using bright satellites like [Echo I](/wiki/Echo_satellite#Echo_1 "Echo satellite"), [Echo II](/wiki/Echo_satellite#Echo_2 "Echo satellite") and [Pageos](/wiki/Pageos "Pageos"), global networks were determined, which later provided support for the theory of [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics").

Another important improvement was the introduction of [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio") and electronic satellites like [Geos A](/wiki/GEOS_(satellite) "GEOS (satellite)") and B (1965–70), of the [Transit](/wiki/Transit_(satellite) "Transit (satellite)") system ([Doppler effect](/wiki/Doppler_effect "Doppler effect")) 1967-1990 — which was the predecessor of GPS - and of [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") techniques like [LAGEOS](/wiki/LAGEOS "LAGEOS") (USA, Italy) or [Starlette](/wiki/Starlette "Starlette") (France). Despite the use of spacecraft, small networks for [cadastral](/wiki/Cadastral "Cadastral") and [technical](/wiki/Technology "Technology") projects are mainly measured terrestrially, but in many cases incorporated in national and global networks by satellite geodesy.

#### Global navigation satellite systems (GNSS)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_control_network&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Global navigation satellite systems (GNSS)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/DGPS_Reference_Station.jpg/250px-DGPS_Reference_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:DGPS_Reference_Station.jpg)

Typical GNSS reference station

See also: [Satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation")

Nowadays, several hundred geospatial satellites are in orbit, including a large number of [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing") satellites and [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") systems like [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") and [Glonass](/wiki/Glonass "Glonass"), which was followed by the European [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") satellites in 2020 and China's [Beidou](/wiki/Beidou "Beidou") [constellation](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation").

While these developments have made satellite-based geodetic network surveying more flexible and cost effective than its terrestrial equivalent for areas free of tree canopy or urban canyons, the continued existence of [fixed point](/wiki/Benchmark_(surveying) "Benchmark (surveying)") networks is still needed for administrative and legal purposes on local and regional scales. Global geodetic networks cannot be defined to be fixed, since [geodynamics](/wiki/Geodynamic "Geodynamic") are continuously changing the position of all [continents](/wiki/Continent "Continent") by 2 to 20 cm per year. Therefore, modern global networks like [ETRS89](/wiki/ETRS89 "ETRS89") or [ITRF](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System "International Terrestrial Reference System") show not only [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate "Coordinate") of their "fixed points", but also their annual [velocities](/wiki/Velocity "Velocity").
